data.title: Kraft Heinz: Institutional Investors Likely Reaching A Tipping Point
data.description:
Sometimes that status quo becomes unacceptable. While there are times that investors will tacitly accept having a bad or underperforming investment in a portfolio because that financial option isn't significantly undermining the overall investing performance of an account, at some point, most people reach a breaking point with these kinds of decisions.

The Kraft Heinz Company (KHC) has been one of the most disappointing companies in the market for nearly a decade now. This retail consumer consistently and significantly underperformed both most peers and the S&P 500 (SPY).

The Kraft Heinz Company has offered investors total returns of 47.80 percent over the last decade, while the S&P 500 has offered investors total returns of 334.2 percent during the same time period.

I last wrote about KHC in August of 2025, and I rated the company a strong sell, primarily because of the company's inability to grow mature brands, on which management relies for earnings and revenues. Today, I am reiterating my strong sell rating. I wanted to revisit my coverage of KHC since the company recently reported earnings and announced a plan to split the food seller's consumer businesses into two companies. Kraft-Heinz has also been relying on what is increasingly clear to be unsustainable price increases, and with most middle- and low-income consumers facing tough choices and a weaker economy, the company can no longer rely on pricing power.

The Kraft Heinz Company's last earnings should raise multiple red flags for investors. Management recently stated that third-quarter earnings per share normalized actual came in at $.61 a share, and revenue actual was $6.24 billion. While the company slightly beat very muted EPS expectations of $.58 a share, management also missed revenue forecasts by $22 million. The company also reported net sales fell by 2.3 percent on a year-to-year basis, and organic net sales fell by 2.5 percent annually. Management also stated that the company's adjusted gross profit margins fell by 200 basis points. While this well-known company was able to achieve some short-term earnings growth by cutting costs, KHC's guidance also shows that the company does not have a clear plan to address the multiple significant issues the leadership team of its food seller is struggling with. The Kraft Heinz Company reiterated that plans to split the company into two companies, Global Taste Elevation and North American Grocery Co., should be completed by the second half of 2026.

KHC gets nearly 75 percent of the company's revenues from more mature markets such as North America, where core brands The Kraft Heinz Company relies heavily on increasingly appear to have reached a point of saturation.

The market share of Heinz is nearly 80 percent in Europe and 60 percent in the US. KHC's Kraft Mac and Cheese brand has nearly 39 percent of this market in the US, down from nearly 45 percent in 2022. Finally, The Kraft Heinz Company's Philadelphia cream cheese brand has nearly two-thirds of this market in the US. KHC's core problem is that the company's brands are not growing in the most important markets in which they operate, and management's pricing power isn't driving earnings or revenue growth anymore either. The decision to break the company into 2 separate entities does not address any of the core issues that KHC has faced for years, which is why Warren Buffett has stated his clear opposition to this decision by KHC's leaders.

The Kraft Heinz Company's revenues and earnings have stagnated for some time; management should be focused on trying to drive more growth outside of North America in addition to developing new brands.

KHC's revenues have actually declined by nearly $1.7 billion since 2016, and the company's earnings growth has slowed significantly as well.

The Kraft Heinz Company has also accumulated a significant amount of debt, and management has recently stated plans to use existing cash flow to reduce these liabilities instead of investing in the company's struggling core brands.

While KHC's current long-term debt level of nearly $19 billion is down from the nearly $31 billion in long-term debt The Kraft Heinz Company was dealing with in 2020 during the pandemic, the company's current market cap is nearly $27 billion.

This is why The Kraft Heinz Company looks overvalued at 8x expected forward EBITDA. Even though KHC's 5-year average valuation is 10.19 forecasted forward EBITDA, the well-known company is seeing negative sales and revenue numbers, as well as anemic earnings growth. Analysts are expecting the company to grow earnings at just 4-6 percent over the next 5 years, and these projections look too optimistic, with 19 of the last earnings revisions being downward, and KHC facing significant challenges growing core brands.

The company is also completing a $3 billion share buyback program, and management will likely be constrained from authorizing new and significant share buybacks because of the company's significant debt levels.

All investment theses have risks, and KHC has brought debt levels down notably without having to reduce dividend payments since 2019. The company's market share and revenues also remain stable, and if forex moves become more favorable, the company will likely see some slight tailwinds from the business operations in Europe and emerging markets. Management may be able to focus more on the core brands after the company downsizes in the second half of 2026, which could be a positive as well, although most of these labels appear to have clearly reached a point of saturation.

When The Kraft Heinz Company reports fourth-quarter earnings in early February, I expect the company's net sales growth to continue to be negative, within the low single-digit range, and I do not believe management will detail a specific plan to reinvest in the company's core brands. The key metric to evaluate will be organic net sales growth, particularly outside of the US. KHC's long-term debt levels, which remain high despite coming down notably, are important to take note of as well. Finally, management has not yet clearly articulated what, if any, significant plans the company has to reinvest or help grow the more mature core products that have struggled to take market share in the main markets KHC operates in.

There are many times when strong brands don't equate to good investments, and The Kraft Heinz Company has not been able to drive any consistent or substantive earnings growth for some time. While the well-known company's core brands are mostly maintaining existing market share levels, management has also not articulated a clear and comprehensive plan to grow market share or expand into new markets either. While The Kraft Heinz Company's dividend should remain stable, investors will likely be able to find better value elsewhere.

data.title: O‑I Glass: Fit‑To‑Win Momentum Sets The Stage For Multi‑Year Upside
data.description:
Introduction

O-I Glass (OI) has the past three months gained strong momentum from successful execution of their " Fit to Win" strategy. With their well structured and solid plan, I believe O-I Glass will have some very successful years ahead, despite volume decline and competitive challenges across Europe and the Americas. And according to my discounted EPS valuation, there is definitely more upside if the plan is executed accordingly.

In this article, I will go through why I believe O-I Glass is a strong buy, and I will start the article by going through what has happened the past couple of months.

What has happened?

OI has been flat since late 2023. This has been driven by falling revenue and declining margins. However, more recently, OI initiated their so-called "Fit to Win" strategy. This involved cutting costs drastically. Their initiative has been quite successful, and the operating margin is now at slightly over 10% TTM.

The first nine months of sales were quite flat compared to 2024. Gross profit is up slightly despite a small decline in revenue. Gross margin improved by 1.11% over 2024, which came from the structural cost cuts. FY 2025 volumes are guided to be down about 2% YoY. In Q3, volumes were down 5%, but management stated that around 3% were noise from inventory corrections due to policy changes.

The past nine months have been driven by their "Fit to Win" strategy. In Q3, Operating profit was up 60%, and more importantly margin rose up by 570 bps. Management targets at least $650m in savings by 2027, and OI is already ahead with estimated savings of around $250 million for the full year of 2025.

OI's plan to reduce more costs is by shutting down furnaces whenever possible, cutting the workforce, and focusing on higher plant utilisation. A way of increasing higher plant utilisation is to shut down furnaces that have low utilisation. The remaining furnaces will then operate on a more optimal capacity. This also lowers the cost per ton, and reduces overhead costs. In total, their plan is to reduce capacity by 13%, as demand is down about 13% since pre-pandemic. Capacity reduction is largely skewed towards Europe, and the last remaining 5% capacity is expected to be shut down by early 2026.

OI's energy contract in Europe is set to expire by the end of 2025. This will have a negative impact as the previous contract was quite favourable. However, further costs reductions will more than offset the higher expected energy rates in Europe.

In America, operating cost reduction and net price were the biggest drivers for margin improvement. Europe's net price declined due to cyclical demand from certain beverages; however, Europe saw the biggest increase in profits due to the huge cost benefits during the quarter. Europe is up 70% YoY, and the Americas are up 59%.

OI costs reduction ahead of schedule has impacted guidance. They expect higher earnings, FCF and reduced leverage ratio from the "Fit to Win" initiative, as they assume most of the restructuring will be finished ahead of time by mid-2026. Volume/mix is projected to be flat throughout 2026, but top-line is expected to grow from improving mix.

Growth

Their "Fit to Win" strategy is centred around making sure OI increases profitability before anything else. They have to remove inefficiencies and cut costs before being able to focus on growth opportunities. It revolves around getting margin and cash growth before volume growth and tougher competition. OI has been losing market share towards Asian imports. This is something they will address when they are close to being finished with the restructuring. The last step is to increase growth is to expand into new markets, there is more regions for OI to enter after OI has addressed their current challenges.

The premiumisation of wine and spirits is a long-term trend. It carries better brand perception and has more presence than its counterparts. More premium bottles carry higher margins, so this trend is identified to be a long-term margin driver for OI. CFO has a target of a 40% premium mix in the foreseeable future. For FY 2025, the premium mix stands at 27%.

New product development, 50% reduction in time to market, 10% of volume is new products. 8-10% growth in new product developments over the year, which is a driver for growth. Clients wants new ideas, and branding power.

Microplastic is a hot topic and a driver towards more glass demand. Growth from non-alcoholic beverages is a realistic growth driver. Consumers' concern about microplastics is pushing demand towards glass.

TOE, or Total Organisation Effectiveness, is a programme that OI has launched that works on improving effectiveness. Their pilot test saw a 10% productivity improvement over a 20-week trial. The goal is to unlock trapped capacity to improve more profitable volume. If this works out in scale, OI could shut down furnaces and get higher utilisation on the active furnaces. This way OI could increase available capacity without major capital investments.

Another driver for earnings growth is lower interest rates. OI is a very leveraged company, with a current debt to equity ratio of more than 300%. And about half of the total debt has a floating rate, a 100 bps cut in interest rate could impact interest expenses by around $30 million. There is chances of rate cuts as inflation is decelerating.

Valuation

Below, I have my discounted EPS valuation. I have made three scenarios based on different growth rates and multiples. Each scenario uses the FWD EPS of $1.61 and a discount rate of 10%, which is the discount rate I use for all my valuations for comparability reasons.

Earnings is projected to grow double‑digits over the next four years. However, I will use a more conservative, steady‑state growth rates in my scenarios to account for the likely deceleration after O‑I’s margin expansion normalizes.

The base case uses a growth rate of 6% for the first five years and 4% for the last five years. This scenario has a multiple of 10x, which is slightly higher than the 5-year average PE ratio. This scenario calculates a share price of $22.

The best case uses a growth rate of 8% for the first five years and 6% for the last five years. This scenario has a multiple of 12x, which is significantly higher then the 5-year average PE ratio, but is justified through the higher growth rate. This scenario calculates a share price of $28.85

The worst case uses a growth rate closer to expected top-line growth in the next couple of years. 4% the first five years and 2% the last five years. This case has a multiple of 8x which is equal to the 5-year average PE ratio. This calculates a share price of $18.37.

Risks

I would categorize OI's main risks into three baskets.

1. Alcohol beverage cyclicality

2. Inflation and energy prices

3. High leverage

Alcohol beverages is experiencing lower demand then pre-pandemic, and it is due to changes by the consumer. OI has classified this as a cyclical reduced demand, and will change over time. However, the depth of this could hurt volumes going forward, and challenge OI top-line growth. There was a change in consumer habits after covid-19. Younger Europeans are drinking less and less alcohol, actually more than 1 in 4 between the age 25-35 have ditched alcohol all together. In the US, only 50% of the age between 18 - 34 say they consume alcohol.

Inflation and energy prices is another challenge. In an uncertain world, trade wars and bottlenecks could drive prices and energy prices, and that will challenge their cost base.

OI is a heavily leveraged company. They have a high debt to equity of more than 300%, this comes with an ICR of 1.8x. This is really low, and something you as an investor has to keep in mind investing in OI. The outlook is higher margins and possibly reduced interest rates, which will drive ICR but there is little margin of safety at this level, so that is important to note.

Conclusion

After a thorough analysis, I believe OI will be a solid investment into 2026 and beyond. They have had a successful start of their "Fit to Win" strategy, and have laid out a solid plan for continued growth the years to come. Despite, lower demand from the beverage market, especially wine and beer, they have other growth drivers that could help volumes and top-line growth, such as new products, higher premiumization mix and growth from non-alcoholic beverages. Also, according to my discounted EPS valuation, the upside is high. However, there is risks but the risk/reward setup is highly attractive to me, which is why I rate O-I Glass a strong buy!

data.title: Build-A-Bear's Weakening Margins Make It Less Compelling (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
My thesis for Build-A-Bear Workshop, Inc. (BBW) is that its stock price is likely to continue rising over the long term because the company will keep buying back its stock. For this to work, however, the company needs to maintain its margins, and right now, tariffs and other costs are pressuring its margins. Build-A-Bear’s growth also slowed down in the third quarter, as expected, and it looks likely to slow down in the fourth quarter as well. While I think my overall thesis for this stock is still correct, because of its recent rally, it doesn’t look like a great deal right now.

On December 4, 2025, Build-A-Bear reported its 3Q 2025 results. Revenue rose 2.7% to $122.7 million, which was lower than expected. Build-A-Bear’s EPS of $0.62 also beat estimates by $0.03 per share, although its EPS was down 11.1% year-over-year. The company’s EBIT margin also fell from 11.0% to 8.7% because of the tariffs. While Build-A-Bear’s stock price fell after this report came out, it rose afterward and is now trading at a higher level.

I previously wrote a bullish article on Build-A-Bear because the company’s guidance implied that its margins were likely to improve in the second half of 2026. The company already included the impact of the tariffs in its guidance, and its stock buybacks also support EPS growth. But Build-A-Bear’s SG&A expenses also rose this quarter, and its stock is now trading at a higher multiple as well. So, there are additional factors to consider if you’re thinking about investing at this price.

Build-A-Bear Is Stabilizing Its Margins by Selling Toys at Higher Price Points

The main thing that stood out to me in the 3Q 2025 earnings report was that Build-A-Bear’s SG&A costs rose by 180 basis points. The company spent more money on ads during the third quarter of 2025 to attract shoppers during the holiday season. Build-A-Bear also had to pay higher wages to its in-store employees because states are increasing their minimum wages, so higher compensation costs also impacted its margins.

I don’t think Build-A-Bear’s SG&A costs are going to drop in 2026. States aren’t going to reduce their minimum wages. Ad agencies probably won’t reduce their prices either. Inflation isn’t going to go into reverse. So, the company will need to convince shoppers to pay higher prices for custom plush toys to maintain its margins. Meanwhile, some shoppers already consider the store’s toys to be expensive. So, it’s worth considering how a toy store that’s known for selling children’s toys can continue to raise its prices in 2026.

On the call, Build-A-Bear CEO Sharon John explained that the company now sells 40% of its toys to teens and adults, who have more money to spend than children. Additionally, Build-A-Bear’s limited-edition Glisten reindeer toy remains popular with collectors, even at a price point of $100. Meanwhile, the company still sells more affordable toys for kids, such as its Mini Beans, for around $10. So, it looks like Build-A-Bear plans to continue selling limited-edition toys to adult collectors who have more money to spend, and this should help it stabilize its margins while keeping some affordable toys available for kids.

Build-A-Bear's Guidance Might Not Include Some Collaborations

Build-A-Bear sells many licensed toys that depend on the popularity of other franchises. In 2024, Universal Studios (CMCSA) released the movie Wicked, which has a story based on the witches from The Wizard of Oz. Build-A-Bear was able to capitalize on the popularity of this movie in 2025 as well as 2024. On the call, an analyst asked Build-A-Bear’s CEO if this was why the company had strong Black Friday results. CEO Sharon John said, “So it's been great because Wicked was more successful than we expected in the first year so we were able to prepare a little bit better for this year. And while it is the tremendous partnership, I really can't look at it and say that's the reason why we drove Black Friday or that's the reason why we're seeing this increase. Our November trend is based on a much broader assortment than that.”

Collaborations like this can drive sales growth if they succeed, but sometimes they don’t succeed, and that disappoints investors. Bath & Body Works (BBWI) underperformed in 3Q 2025 because its Disney (DIS) Villains collaboration wasn’t as successful as expected, and now Bath & Body Works doesn’t want to use collaborations to carry upcoming quarters. It sounds like the Build-A-Bear CEO doesn’t want to do this either because the results of a collaboration can be hard to predict. So, Build-A-Bear’s guidance might not include some of the potential gains from upcoming collaborations.

My Updated 4Q 2025 Expectations For Build-A-Bear

Build-A-Bear has now reported cumulative revenue of $375.3 million and net income of $35.8 million for the first 3 quarters of 2025. And it maintained its full-year guidance for mid-to-high single-digit revenue growth. This implies that management expects strong 4Q results. In my previous article, I projected that Build-A-Bear’s revenue would rise 8% to $536.1 million in 2025 based on its full-year guidance. To reach that goal, the company would need revenue of $160.8 million in 4Q 2025. Its 4Q 2024 revenue was $150.4 million, so it would need 6.9% revenue growth to hit that target. That still looks achievable if its 3Q report was an outlier.

Build-A-Bear’s third quarter ended on November 1, 2025, so its third-quarter results don’t include its results from November. On the call, the CFO said, “And as we talked about, we had a softer finish to the Q3, but we rebounded in November, and we are seeing some positive momentum. We, as Sharon talked about, the best Black Friday in our history.” So, it looks like the company does have preliminary evidence that its revenue growth will be higher in the fourth quarter, and this explains why it maintained its guidance.

Relying On International Expansion Is A Risk

Build-A-Bear is a relatively unique concept. Many US cities may not be able to support an upscale toy store, so this store may have less domestic expansion potential than other stores. It looks like the company plans to continue growing by having franchise owners open new stores in foreign countries. This is an asset-light growth strategy that probably sounds impressive to investors. But it’s possible that the Build-A-Bear concept won’t work as well in some foreign countries, and Build-A-Bear is adding stores in many new countries at the same time.

Valuation Update For Build-A-Bear

Again, if Build-A-Bear averages 8% revenue growth for the year, its full-year 2025 revenue will be $536.1 million. I made this estimate in my previous article, and the company maintained its guidance this quarter. Build-A-Bear also called for $66 million in EBIT and a 23% tax rate at the midpoint of its estimates, which would result in full-year income of $50.8 million. So, it would post $15.0 million in income in 4Q 2025, and it would have an 8.8% full-year profit margin. Build-A-Bear’s TTM profit margin is 10.9%, but its profit margin fell to 6.6% in the third quarter because of tariffs.

It looks like my original estimate for Build-A-Bear’s 2026 revenue growth may have been too low because its third-quarter results are likely to be an outlier. Management’s remarks on the call suggest that it faced a tougher-than-usual comparison in the third quarter. But the company’s margins are also being eroded by tariffs and rising SG&A costs that will continue next year.

If I project that Build-A-Bear will post 8% revenue growth in 2026, its revenue will rise to $579.0 million. With an 8.8% full-year profit margin, it would report $51 million in income. At a 2026 P/E of 16, the company would have a market cap of $816 million. Build-A-Bear has a lower P/E than the average consumer discretionary stock, with a median P/E of 19, but its forward P/S of 1.55 is higher than the sector median of 0.98. This is a unique company, so it’s hard to find similar stocks to compare it to, but it appears to be reasonably valued in comparison to its sector.

In my previous article, I also projected that Build-A-Bear will have a diluted share count of 12.9 million shares in 2026 because I expect its buybacks to continue. If I divide a market cap of $816 million by 12.9 million shares, I get a target price of $63. Build-A-Bear’s share price after the market close on January 21 was $65, so my target price is 3% below the current price. I’d say that Build-A-Bear looks fairly valued now. The SA Quant score also says that Build-A-Bear is a hold right now.

Conclusion

It may be time to take profit on Build-A-Bear. This company’s stock price has rallied since its third-quarter results came out in early December. While I think that my original estimate for Build-A-Bear’s revenue growth in 2026 was too low, and my estimate for its profit margin in 2026 was too high, the company’s stock price now reflects current expectations. Build-A-Bear’s guidance implies that its revenue growth will accelerate, but its margins may remain weak. So, I’d say that Build-A-Bear is now a hold.

data.title: Palantir: The Valuation Debate Misses A Critical Signal
data.description:
Palantir Technologies Inc. (PLTR) has proven exceptional execution consistency through 2025, as highlighted through its consecutive quarters of earnings outperformance, alongside repeated upward guidance revisions. As discussed in my previous coverage on the stock, Palantir’s results do not just reflect benefits of emerging secular AI demand tailwinds, but also the compounding benefit of its optimized unit economics enabled through AI Platform’s (“AIP”) highly scalable go-to-market strategy. Specifically, the combination of AIP Bootcamp’s low cost, high conversion sales and onboarding strategy, alongside the product’s accelerated time-to-value enabled by Palantir’s ontology have been key drivers of sustained operating leverage improvements and scaled margin expansion in recent years.

Yet the stock’s subsequent appreciation of nearly 200% from 2025’s trough to peak has triggered a great valuation debate, drawing questions on the durability of its current premium at 65x NTM sales and 167x NTM earnings. Consistent with my previous discussion, I believe Palantir’s current valuation continues to accurately reflect its restored 30%+ multi-year growth trajectory, reinforced by a better-than-expected earnings outlook that’s set to compound further as AIP’s superior unit economics continue to scale.

And the durability of this fundamental profile remains intact. Specifically, Palantir’s historical revenue inflection points have been preceded by acceleration in remaining performance obligations (“RPO”). The trend reflects sustained backlog expansion and reinforces accelerating conversion into realized sales. And that relationship persists today, with complementary support from accelerating total contract value (“TCV”) bookings, billings growth, and total deal value expansion.

This effectively reinforces Palantir’s growth visibility and demand durability, which remains underappreciated – especially after the stock’s pullback since late December, which coincided with broader market observations of capital rotation out of tech and software, in particular. Looking ahead, Palantir’s upcoming Q4 earnings update will be pivotal as it kicks off expectations for the current year and beyond. Investors’ focus will likely dwell less on past performance, and instead shift towards whether its forward growth and earnings profile can keep up with the accelerating pace of accretion delivered thus far. And based on historical trends, another earnings surprise likely awaits, underscoring a potential valuation re-rate higher from current levels.

A Year of Consistent Outperformance

PLTR stock’s 2025 upsurge is differentiated by its earnings compounding power. Specifically, Palantir’s average 2025 growth estimate of about 54% is outpaced by comparable software peer Clearwater Analytics’ (CWAN) 62%, with both demonstrating a similar long-term trajectory of expansion. Yet the Palantir stock currently trades at about 65x NTM sales, while CWAN’s valued at a much lower 8x NTM sales. The drastic gap is likely explained by differences in their respective earnings prospects. Clearwater Analytics’ earnings is expected to compound at a five-year CAGR of 27.5% through 2030, while Palantir’s at 35% over the same period. Specifically, Palantir is expected to have achieved close to 77% of earning growth in 2025, with further accretion at the 40% range over the next two years, highlighting a significant scale advantage over its comparable peers, as AIP deployments ramp.

The company’s underlying business performance has effectively become a display of strong execution consistency. This is corroborated by Palantir’s repeatedly raised full year 2025 guidance in the past three earnings updates, which weren’t conservative to begin with. Specifically, management had initially expected Palantir’s 2025 revenue growth to accelerate towards 31% during the February earnings update; this was gradually increased to 54% by the time it delivered Q3 results in November.

While this consistency has reinforced confidence in Palantir’s 30%+ multi-year growth trajectory and played a critical role in driving the stock’s upsurge towards its all-time high in early November, the shares have yet to fully recover from the post-Q3 earnings pullback since.

This trend accordingly highlights a “great valuation debate,” as investors remain cautious over the durability of Palantir’s elevated valuation multiples – especially given broader market dynamics have turned less favorable for tech and software, in particular. Specifically, tech valuations have been volatile since the S&P 500 (SP500) and Nasdaq 100’s (NDX) late October peak, with some of their gains being displaced by names across industrials and big pharma since.

Despite signs of broader market recovery in late December and through the first few weeks of 2026, the tech-heavy Nasdaq 100’s losses remain at more than 4% since its October 29 peak (vs. S&P 500’s -1.36%), representing a cautionary tale for tech valuations ahead. This accordingly suggests heightened investor scrutiny heading into Palantir’s upcoming Q4 earnings update. Specifically, focus is likely to zero-in on whether Palantir can maintain its pace of revenue acceleration and earnings accretion in the year ahead to justify estimates currently embedded into its premium.

And there are several indicators that continue to point to durability on that front:

1. RPO Growth Signals Continued Acceleration

Remaining performance obligations (“RPO”) currently represents one of Palantir’s strongest and most reliable gauge of future revenue momentum. Different from remaining deal value (“RDV”), which “presumes the exercise of all contract options and no termination of contracts” across both of its core government and commercial customers, RPO doesn’t account for “contracts with an initial term of less than 12 months and contractual obligations that fall beyond termination for convenience clauses.” Specifically, management’s repeatedly reminded investors that RPO consists primarily of commercial contracts, as government awards are typically subject to termination clauses and wide-ranging fulfilment timelines.

As a result, RPO trends have historically been a reliable barometer of revenue visibility for Palantir, as they represent confirmed contractual obligations with high conversion certainty. Meanwhile, changes in deal value function as more of a directional indicator of forward growth prospects, given conversion of the full amount to revenue isn’t always guaranteed.

Historically, Palantir’s revenue inflections have been preceded by accelerating RPO, as sustained backlog expansion underscores rapid conversion into realized sales in subsequent periods. In the past 12 months, there have been constructive patterns observed between total and current RPO expansion and their subsequent period of commercial revenue growth, in particular.

As depicted by red arrows in the image above, growth observed in quarterly current RPO through 2024 has demonstrated close correlation with commercial revenue growth in the immediately subsequent period. Their differentials averaged less than 1.5 percentage points. With the assistance of generative AI (Grok and ChatGPT), the data translated to strong positive correlation of 0.828. This is consistent with earlier discussions that RPO is primarily reflective of commercial customer contracts, given their longer duration and lower rate of subjection to termination clauses.

Yet in the early half of 2025, there’s been a shift in the pattern where total RPO growth has displayed greater correlation with subsequent period commercial revenue growth (depicted by blue arrows). Specifically, differentials between Q1 to Q2 RPO growth and Q2 to Q3 commercial revenue growth averaged about 2.4 percentage points, with strong statistical positive correlation.

This shift, though potentially at early stages given limited data, could potentially indicate favorable changes in Palantir’s RPO composition and deal dynamics, reinforcing its revenue visibility ahead:

Contract duration has likely increased: Historically, Palantir’s RPO has been skewed to current contracts realizable into revenue within 12 months. This is consistent with the fact that it was still in early stages of its land-and-expand growth model for its product portfolio, including AIP introduced in 2023. The dynamic suggests Palantir’s exposure to a larger mix of smaller and shorter duration contracts, which typically signals faster conversion to revenue. This would also be consistent with strong correlation observed between current RPO and forward commercial revenue growth observed through 2024, as discussed earlier.

Based on the Q3 2025 10-Q, more than 56% of Palantir’s RPO are now non-current. In other words, Palantir’s largely guaranteed backlog is now predominantly stemming from long-term contracts that aren’t expected to be fulfilled and recognized into revenue until more than a year later. This compares to a small non-current mix at 54% in Q3 2024, and 47% in Q1 2024 when RPO started growing again following the extended macro headwinds experienced through 2023. The company also continued to acquire bigger contracts in Q3, with 83 new deals worth $1 million or more; out of which, 40 had contract values exceeding $5 million, and 21 of them exceeding $10 million.

With deal size and contract duration on the rise, revenue recognized in the immediately following quarter is increasingly driven by Palantir’s aggregative backlog (i.e., total RPO), rather than just what’s concentrated within the “current” bucket. This would be consistent with increasing correlation between Palantir’s total RPO growth and subsequent quarter commercial revenue growth observed since the beginning of 2025.

Land-and-expand is working: Management has recently disclosed that AIP remains a consistent driver of existing customer deal expansions, as well as new customer conversions – especially in the U.S. This is corroborated by 45% y/y growth in Palantir’s customer count to 911 in Q3, as well as 38% y/y growth in LTM revenue generated from its top 20 customers to $83 million per customer. This collectively corroborates Palantir’s consistent execution on its land-and-expand business model, with revenue driven by both additional customer deployments and newly acquired conversions.

As AIP adoption rises, especially in expansion-led deployments, related growth will become increasingly reflected in the form of contract extensions and incremental workloads. These are dynamics that can’t be adequately captured by current RPO alone, as less of the expanding contract value is expected to be recognized within a 12-month period. Instead, a greater mix of contracted backlog now resides in total RPO.

As a result, forward revenue recognition will become increasingly tied to the absolute increase in customer contract values instead of how fast those relationships can be converted into sales. This dynamic shift effectively reduces the dependence of near-term commercial revenue growth on current RPO alone, and diversifies its exposure to the scale of total contracted backlog.

Taken together, total RPO is likely emerging as the more informative indicator of Palantir’s forward sales conversion trajectory, especially for its commercial segment. As total RPO continues to demonstrate y/y acceleration, it reinforces Palantir’s longer-term revenue visibility, and strengthens durability to the multi-year growth expectations embedded within its current valuation premium.

Specifically, Palantir reported 66% y/y RPO growth. Considering the historical differential of about 2.4 percentage points observed between RPO growth and subsequent quarter commercial revenue sales growth, the pattern could imply Q4 commercial revenue sales growth of at least 63.6% to 68.4%. Coupled with forward government revenue projections in line with consensus estimates, Palantir could achieve full year 2025 revenue of at least $4.4 billion. This would be consistent with management’s raised full year 2025 revenue guidance of $4.396 billion to $4.4 billion provided during the Q3 earnings update.

Taken together, Palantir’s current valuation premium, which prices in a 30%+ multi-year growth trajectory, as discussed in my previous coverage, likely remains intact and substantiated by tangible forward revenue visibility.

2. Adjacent Growth Reinforcements

The durability of Palantir’s RPO acceleration, a critical precursor to sustaining its elevated revenue growth and earnings accretion trajectory, is further reinforced by strength observed across adjacent key performance indicators.

This includes strong acceleration observed in Palantir’s RDV, which grew 91% y/y to $8.6 billion in Q3. As discussed in the earlier section, Palantir’s RDV differs from RPO in that it also includes customer contracts with duration of less than 12 months and contracts that are subject to termination clauses, and presumes exercise of all contract options. As a result, RDV has historically been a key directional indicator of forward demand, rather than a tangible proxy for contracted revenue visibility.

However, it’s likely that Palantir’s RDV will become closer linked with RPO growth going forward, considering Palantir’s evolving end-market demand dynamics. Specifically on the commercial front, accelerating AIP adoption continues to underpin larger and longer duration contract wins. This effectively reduces the prevalence of contracts with “original terms of 12 months or less,” which aren’t included in RPO. Meanwhile, Palantir’s resonation with the Trump administration’s increasing prioritization of defense tech modernization efforts also reinforces its reach into larger, longer-dated government contracts. These end-market demand dynamics collectively underscore increased RDV inclusion in RPO going forward.

Meanwhile, Palantir’s total contract value (“TCV”) bookings are also expanding rapidly, up 151% y/y to $2.8 billion in Q3. Taken together, the strength observed in elevated RDV and TCV bookings growth is expected to reinforce RPO acceleration and, by extension, Palantir’s forward sales conversion and revenue expansion prospects. The growth dynamics also reinforce structural long-term demand for Palantir’s portfolio rather than speculative sentiment driven by secular AI optimism, and reflects the company’s increasingly mission-critical role in serving next-generation AI data analytics needs. This accordingly bolsters durability to the 30%+ multi-year growth trajectory that currently underpins Palantir’s valuation premium.

3. Competitive Edge Led by AIP’s Expanding Breadth

Palantir’s recent expansion of AIP-native features are also expected to accommodate its land-and-expand business model by deepening monetization across the product’s installed base. This could provide incremental reinforcement to the pace of acceleration currently observed across its key growth indicators, and further preserve long-term revenue visibility.

These features include AI FDE, an AIP-native agent that can “translate natural language requests into Foundry operations.” Specifically, AI FDE is capable of performing data transformations and interacting with data within Foundry. It can also help customers build and maintain ontologies, and develop applications and/or drive critical decision-making insights from them. During the Q3 earnings update, management highlighted AI FDE’s differentiated value proposition in enabling substantial productivity gains for existing AIP customers:

At one customer, 2 human FDEs spawned an army of AI FDEs to migrate a customer off their legacy data warehouse in 5 days, something that would have taken an army of SIs up to 2 years. This is not a prototype, this is production. Across our customers, the results are shocking.

Source: Palantir Q3 2025 Earnings Call Transcript.

According to Palantir’s product documentation, AI FDE is currently in the “beta phase of development,” underscoring additive growth prospects when the add-on AIP feature enters general availability.

Similarly, AI Hivemind is another agentic solution aimed at extending AIP’s value proposition across both government and commercial settings. AI Hivemind is an AIP-native feature capable of leading “dynamically generated agents” to engage in solving complex problems and executing orders based on customers’ data ontologies. According to management, AI Hivemind was originally developed to “solve extremely complex problems in the classified space,” and is natively integrated in Palantir’s Gaia application within the government-centric Gotham operating system. Now the feature is expanded for use in commercial settings, identifying and solving enterprise problems like supply chain bottlenecks by collaborating with AI FDE.

Meanwhile, Palantir’s also rolling out Edge Ontology – a “lightweight implementation” of its proprietary ontology optimized for running on mobile devices. As discussed in one of my previous notes on Palantir, the company’s proprietary development of the ontology has been its most prized possession. It’s essentially the core engine that enables rapid scaling of Foundry, Gotham and AIP’s time-to-value for customers. And the edge-optimized version of this solution is expected to reinforce Palantir’s reach into long-term monetization opportunities as compute dynamics evolve. Specifically, the advent of AI has fueled the diversification of enterprise compute dynamics from centralized cloud workloads to distributed deployments on edge devices, spanning autonomous systems, drones, robotics and industrial equipment. This accordingly makes Edge Ontology a critical reinforcement to the long-term durability of Palantir’s elevated RDV, TCV and RPO growth, further underpinning its forward revenue visibility and ensuing margin expansion at scale.

Fundamental and Price Considerations

Taken together, I’m maintaining my base case price target for Palantir at $180 per share.

The price target is computed under a discounted cash flow analysis on base case fundamental projections spanning 2026 to 2035. The forecast reflects revenue expansion from 2026 at a five- and nine-year CAGR of 34% and 29%, respectively, alongside earnings accretion at a five- and nine-year CAGR of 43% and 35%, respectively. The fundamental assumptions considered are consistent with my foregoing analysis on the durability of Palantir’s near-term revenue acceleration prospect, as well as its longer-term growth and earnings accretion trajectory.

Meanwhile, key valuation assumptions applied include a perpetual growth rate of 3.5% applied on terminal value, proxied by estimated 2035 EBITDA, in determining Palantir’s terminal value. The perpetual growth rate assumption is consistent with the anticipated pace of economic expansion across Palantir’s core operating markets and reflects the rate at which the company can sustain long-term FCF accretion as the business matures. Total cash flow projections, including the terminal value, is discounted at a WACC of 8.8%, which is in line with Palantir’s capital structure and risk profile.

What Can Go Wrong?

A key risk overhang facing Palantir is its increasing involvement in political controversy. From day one, CEO Alex Karp has stated in a letter embedded within Palantir’s Form S-1 filing that the company has “chosen sides” and will only “support Western liberal democracy and its strategic allies.” While Palantir’s government segment is largely de-risked from its strategic mission, the commercial segment remains prone. Specifically, existing and prospective enterprise customers – including those based in the U.S. and allied countries – may not always resonate and align with Palantir’s political views.

This includes recent attention on Palantir’s ongoing work with the ICE and the Defense Department amid escalations in both domestic affairs and geopolitical tensions. Karp responded that these relationships “have provoked protests inside and outside the company,” highlighting potential risks to operations. The implications are also clearly acknowledged in the business and industry risks outlined in Palantir’s latest 10-K (emphasis in original):

Our reputation and business may be harmed by news or social media coverage of Palantir or our leadership, including but not limited to coverage that presents, or relies on, inaccurate, misleading, incomplete, or otherwise damaging information.

...Conversely, being perceived as yielding to activism targeted at certain customers could damage our relationships with certain customers, including governments and government agencies with which we do business, whose views may or may not be aligned with those of political and social activists.

We may not enter into relationships with potential customers if we consider their activities to be inconsistent with our organizational mission or values.

...Although we endeavor to do business with customers and governments that are aligned with our mission and values, we cannot predict how the activities and values of our government and private sector customers will evolve over time, and they may evolve in a manner inconsistent with our mission.

Source: Palantir 2024 10-K.

Another potential risk that could disrupt Palantir’s growth trajectory are rising risks of talent drain. Specifically, the Trump administration’s pro-defense tech agenda has encouraged an upsurge of start-ups similar to Palantir. Aside from competition, which I believe Palantir’s technology and business model continues to hold a deep moat against, the emerging start-ups could risk disrupting the company’s talent composition, which is critical to maintaining its current scale. This is reflected in management’s acknowledgement that investing in “the most elite technical talent” remains a strategic priority for Palantir. It’s also corroborated by risks elaborated in Palantir’s latest 10-K filing:

If we are unable to hire, retain, train, and motivate qualified personnel and senior management, including Alexander Karp, one of our founders and our Chief Executive Officer, and deploy our personnel and resources to meet customer demand around the world, our business could suffer.

Source: Palantir 2024 10-K.

Palantir’s commitment to preventing talent drain is also reflected in the company’s recent lawsuit against certain ex-employees. The company claimed that the ex-employees have allegedly violated non-compete clauses and “improperly poached Palantir employees and stole confidential documents,” which the defendants have called a “scare tactic.”

The combination would not only risk driving up near-term legal expenses for Palantir, but also expose the company to higher personnel costs to retain talent. This could suggest risks of resurgent stock-based compensation expense, which has previously been a drag on its margins. Any added pressure to Palantir’s near-term profitability could potentially weaken durability of the stock’s current premium.

Final Thoughts

Taken together, Palantir looks well positioned for a potential upside surprise on both the Q4 financial results and 2026 outlook heading into its upcoming earnings update. This strength is not dependent on surface secular AI tailwinds, but rather rooted in tangible execution consistency, with durability reflected in sustained acceleration across its key growth indicators.

The company’s increasing mix of expansion-led growth is also expected to reinforce sustained margin expansion at scale and drive earnings accretion critical to the cash flows underpinning its valuation. This is further reinforced by Palantir’s pivot to expanding the breadth of AIP-native features, which will bolster long-term monetization and compound growth stemming from installed base expansion.

Looking ahead, the 30%+ multi-year growth trajectory embedded in Palantir’s premium remains intact. With its upcoming Q4 earnings update likely to set the tone for 2026 expectations, an upside surprise could further lift its growth and earnings profile, potentially underscoring a re-rate for PLTR stock from current levels.

data.title: Oklo: Meta Partnership Excites Investors, But Doesn't Fix The Fundamentals
data.description:
Oklo (OKLO) is a small nuclear reactor developer company that has recently captured investors' attention as it said that it plans for ambitious developments of nuclear reactors and a historic power agreement with Meta Platforms (META). The deal is based on this new corporate support and Oklo's pioneering compact fission technology, which is an integral part of this company.

Nevertheless, Oklo is an early-stage development company that is still experiencing high costs of operation and regulatory barriers. There is no doubt that the Meta agreement is a first step towards validation of the company at a high level, which could be useful in de-risking long-term projects of Oklo. And investors also took this news positively, which made the stock jump about 8%. However, the big risk is that the company is not profitable and is burning cash. So the capital requirement is substantial, and the valuation is very high, making the expectation of any future growth very high.

This analysis will discuss the nature of the Meta deal, the financial performance of Oklo, the balance sheet and capital expenditure situation, policy, and valuation in comparison to their peers to determine whether the optimism is justified today or not.

Meta Deal Supports Oklo

So from this deal, it's clear that Oklo intends to build an Ohio microreactor centre at Aurora. The latest collaboration with Meta Platforms will finance the initial construction of the 1.2GW Oklo nuclear facility. Under the deal, Meta will shotgun the construction of power, which would technically underwrite the initial stage of the Oklo build-out.

In addition, Reuters said that by 2030, up to 1.2 GW of energy will be developed in Ohio with the assistance of Meta. The company will facilitate the purchasing and development of Oklo reactors early.

If we see this practically, Meta will allow Oklo to place fuel and equipment orders and start site work on the Aurora units because of its upfront capital.

According to Oklo CEO Jacob DeWitte, "This funding commitment is the largest leap towards nuclear technology, and Oklo can gain fuel and take Phase 1 of the Pike County project".

So it looks like the Meta arrangement practically makes Oklo a patient customer and part-patient financier, like a power-purchase scheme, but of reactors that are yet to be developed.

But here is the reality check: although this is a solid validation, real revenue will not come in for several years to come. By 2034, the largest commitment, under 1.2 GW, will be made, and by 2030, only conceptual milestones.

So in my opinion, the agreement is a good recommendation, causing a reduction in the risk of financing, but it has no immediate impact on the cash flow of Oklo and its regulatory clock.

Business Model and Fundamentals

Oklo is a small recruit in the giant nuclear industry in the vast technology sector. It primarily has the Aurora fast-spectrum microreactor, a modular nuclear power plant capable of producing approximately 77 MW per unit, and can be expanded by adding additional modules.

But here is the twist: The main thing that makes the plants at Oklo different from the old-fashioned reactors is that the Oklo plants are bite-sized and made of high-assay and low-enriched uranium (high-assay low-enriched uranium, or HALEU) and are passively cooled. They are also the proprietors of Atomic Alchemy, a company that manufactures radioisotopes that are used in medicine and industry.

But investors should know that to date, Oklo reactors have not been switched on, and they have not been selling any power yet. They are stagnated in the research and development and licensing, and the revenues they record so far are practically none.

Briefly, we can say that Oklo is pre-commercial, having no electric sales only limited revenue from its technology.

The company raises funds in the books, which are all grants or R&D money, so 100% of sales have been crashing down at Oklo. Moreover, operations costs have exploded in the last two years since it attracted their attention following an SPAC deal in mid-2023, as Oklo works to assemble engineering teams and pursue licenses. Additionally, we have seen R&D and G&A nearly triple during the first 9 months of 2025 compared to the year before.

According to the Q3 2025 results, the R&D expenditure experienced an increase of 76% over the last year, whereas the G&A expenses increased by 196%. The 9-month operating expense in 2025 was $82.2 million compared to $61.3 million the previous year, and the net loss was $64.2 million, compared to the $61.3 million the previous year, 4.8%. Increased expenditure with a smaller increase in loss boils down to interest and tax stuff, which consumes part of the burn.

In short, Oklo is not a profitable company; it is heavily burning cash in research. The operating loss and revenue were approximately $36.3 million in the last quarter, which is virtually nothing.

There is low capital expenditure based on the R&D burn. They possess approximately $11.5 million in net property and equipment, which is to be used in early site prep and equipment. They are, mostly, stashing cash in marketable securities rather than in physical plants. As long as they have cash sitting in the treasuries, they have not been spending it on reactor hardware. Capex will increase rapidly when, in fact, they begin to construct Aurora units. Financials are, so far, purely R&D, and the capital is mainly in financial form.

The balance sheet of Oklo is liquid and clean, and there is no revenue-generating asset. By Q3, they had up to $410 million in cash and as much as $772 million in short-term securities and a total of $1.18 billion in liquid assets- thanks partially to a more recent equity round in 2024-25. The total assets consisted of approximately $1.25 billion, and the liabilities were only $40.6 million. Their debt is negligible; the only large charge is a right of first refusal of $25 million, not a real estate acquisition.

Overall, Oklo does not have any commercial sales, puts money into investments, and keeps cash in equities. The profitability will not come until the point when they sell reactor tech or begin selling isotopes such as meitnerium and radium.

Policy Tailwinds and Regulatory Landscape

The one most important thing about energy companies like Oklo is that they rely heavily on the nuclear policy in the U.S. and the speed at which regulators will respond, and events have begun to become more favourable.

For example, in mid-2025, the Trump administration had passed executive orders that accelerated the approval of advanced reactors and increased the production of nuclear fuels in the U.S. That made the DOE and NRC relax the rules of licensing and establish definite schedules.

Oklo is so excited about the move, particularly following its initial license rush when its initial attempt was to be held up by an arduous, tedious procedure, which also delayed the subsidiary license effort. The support has now become bipartisan, and Congress has instructed NRC to establish a special path to such reactors.

But there's still some risk these policies carry, like Oklo, which requires a complete green light so that it can construct and operate its reactors, even with the friendlier policy. Investors should also note that these are long-term projects that may require years, and any diversion in the political attention or failure to get support may lead to hiccups.

Comparison with Peers and Valuation Metrics

Oklo stock is currently trading at some premium levels compared to its peers. The company has a market cap of around $16 to $17 billion at the beginning of 2026, despite the fact that the company has failed to secure any form of revenue or operating power plants.

At the same time, the experienced utility giant Vistra (VST) is trading at almost the same valuation, but the difference is that it generates up to $17 billion in annual income and nearly $1 billion in earnings.

Another major peer is NuScale (SMR), which is a sister company with NRC-approved advanced-reactor concepts, trading at a value of around $5.8 billion, and also promised to generate less than $300 billion in revenue by 2027.

At the same multiple, Oklo would have to reach approximately $800 million to $1 billion a year in revenue in several years to support its price tag, which is quite an uphill task.

Conclusion

I agree that Oklo's recent deal with Meta is an important milestone that will capitalize and introduce its initial projects with a ready customer. Plus, it is also a validation that big companies are taking an interest in nuclear-powered AI. Nevertheless, Oklo should not base its investment case on one deal. In essence, Oklo is a deep-technology company that has not earned any income or made a complete reactor.

We have seen in recent quarters that the losses and burn of cash have been increasing immensely as the company increases its staffing and engages in R&D. Oklo plans to continue to tap that capital over the successful years despite having a strong balance sheet due to its equity raises.

The only case for rewards, which I think can be enormous if Oklo assembles a group of reactors within budget and on time. However, the setbacks, technological hitches, and lack of cash are equally major headaches. Oklo is thus a nice speculative bet in the nuclear world that is already made, rather than a safe short-term option.

The investor who is willing to bet on this stock must keep in mind that the dream is bigger and the accomplishment is a long and arduous task.

data.title: Nvidia: The New Boeing In China
data.description:
In December, I discussed the then-upcoming approval of Nvidia Corporation's (NVDA) H200 chip exports to China. At the time, I pointed out that sales to China were highly uncertain and implementing higher sales expectations driven by increased sales to China would be premature. The latest news is that China is not accepting shipments of the advanced H200 chips. In this report, I discuss why that might be the case and how Nvidia is becoming the new Boeing (BA) in the trade turmoil.

The Revenue Sharing Agreement That Wasn't

Initially, it seemed as if Nvidia had reached an agreement to ship H200 sales to China; As part of a revenue sharing agreement, China would get access to the advanced H200 chip which was a step up from the dumbed down version of H20 developed for the Chinese market in exchange for the U.S. Government taking a 25% cut.

On the 14th of January, the outline of the mechanism was announced. H200 chips would not be directly shipped to China from the production location but first to the U.S. Upon entry in the U.S., a 25% tariff would be applied to be paid by the importing party in the U.S. After that, the H200 could be shipped to China.

A true revenue sharing agreement would reduce Nvidia’s revenues, as the customer would pay $25 if we hypothetically assume a sale of $100 worth of H200 chips at a cost of good of $40. It would imply margins of 46.7%. If Nvidia were to charge a higher price essentially passing through the tariff, it would pay 25% based on the $100 worth of revenues that is being imported to the U.S. before exporting it, but that is not a revenue item as the company would merely serve as the collecting agent and the passing through is settled outside of the P&L. As a result, the $25 is fully paid for by the customer. If Nvidia were to absorb the higher tariff, it would essentially have the $25 tariff as an expense, as gross margins would drop to 35%.

So, from an accounting perspective, these are all different margin scenarios. We also note that the current 25% levy whether passed through or absorbed by Nvidia is fundamentally different in timing. The revenue share would trigger once a sale occurs, while the pass-through and tariff absorption have the $25 fee applied upon entry into the U.S. Revenue sharing would seem like a perfect tool for President Trump with a business approach. However, it would effectively be a forced royalty or export tax, which would not only be bad for Nvidia’s top line but also be violating Article I, Section 9, Clause 5 Exports:

No Tax or Duty shall be laid on Articles exported from any State.

Whether Nvidia is passing through the levy that it pays on import is not known, but what is known is that what currently is being executed is not a revenue sharing agreement. Perhaps that provides a hurdle for China to accept H200 sales, but it is also possible that the country is maximizing leverage or stalling a chip that at this moment they do not need, also to increase leverage. While Nvidia does not bake in sales to China for the H200 in its guidance, the block by China is painful as Nvidia had upside for 1 million orders valued $30 billion.

Painful Parallels With Boeing

The fact that China now refuses a product cleared by the U.S. reminds me in some ways of the Boeing 737 MAX. During the trade war initiated during President Trump’s first term, China retaliated by imposing tariffs on U.S. airplanes. The country, though, carefully sculpted the weight limits such that it would actually not obstruct deliveries of the Boeing 737 MAX.

However, after the second crash of a MAX in 2019 the country was quick to ground the Boeing 737 MAX, and only ungrounded the MAX once demand forced the country to do so and paced fleet reactivations and new deliveries in line with demand. The Boeing 737 MAX had essentially become the playball of trade politics and while the situation with the H200 is different, we are seeing that yet again China is making a U.S. product the playball of trade politics. This is regulatory gatekeeping as leverage. In that regard, I also believe it is a good step that Nvidia may have slowed down H200 and even halted production:

Suppliers of parts for Nvidia’s H200 have paused production after Chinese customs officials blocked shipments of the newly approved artificial intelligence processors from entering China.

In 2025, Boeing deliveries to China were briefly grounded, resulting in a temporary increase in inventory. For the H200 chip, it is extremely important that Nvidia does not produce the chip in large quantities if the Chinese market does not open up soon as H200 is based on the older Hopper architecture while the current standard architecture for global sales is Blackwell and the Rubin/Vera Rubin is set to launch in late 2026. Continuing production would substantially increase inventory and the risk of write-offs, given the limited appeal of the H200 in other markets.

The Best Expectation Is To Not Expect Sales To China

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

Setting a price target for Nvidia stock can be challenging. I believe a low-end price target would be $201.54 in line with the peer group and 2027 earnings in mind. The base case would be $243.53 based on 2026 earnings indicating 33% upside and more optimistically we would stick with a peer group valuation and shift our focus from FY27 to FY28 as the company is heading into FY27 and looking one year ahead from there we can pull FY28 into the estimates.

I previously also established a price target based on the average implied price targets for 2027 and that would now indicate a $303.20 price target with 65% upside. However, under the current circumstances, that is not a price target that, I believe, is achievable unless there will be positive news on sales to China. What I do like about the current valuation is that there is upside based on forecast earnings for FY27, regardless of whether the company or peer group EV/EBITDA multiple is used.

For Nvidia, we are actually seeing a high degree of stability in the growth. That may sound weird. However, we continue to see strong margins, cash conversion north of 70%, low CapEx against operating cash flow and quasi-linear growth expectations for free cash flow driving the cash position to $200 billion by 2028, no net debt and gross debt being extremely low against the EBITDA performance.

Conclusion: Nvidia Remains Attractive Even Without China

Approval of sales in China would be a catalyst for Nvidia share prices. However, like I concluded in my prior report there is a lot of uncertainty regarding H200 chip sales to China and my fear remains that the approval of deliveries to China has come little to late in the cycle and China is now in the position to make the H200 chips part of the trade tension in the same way it has done with the Boeing 737 MAX. That would be unfortunate for Nvidia, but we note that the stock now trades at discount to peers without sales expectations for China. Other semiconductor names have surged while Nvidia stock has lagged behind and that potentially provides some safer upside for Nvidia shares.

data.title: Suncor: Moving My Profit-Taking Target
data.description:
Investment thesis.

Given a number of fundamental oil market trends, coupled with broader stock market dynamics, I decided to shift my starting point for profit taking on my Suncor (SU) shares, which make up the largest stock position in my portfolio. i am also increasing my buy target.

From a supply/demand oil market perspective, 2025 ended with a convergence of factors that may have created a new, perhaps permanent all-time high for global crude oil supplies. US crude oil output increased by about 700,000 b/d from the start of 2025 to the end. At the same time, the OPEC+ group added about 2 mb/d in output last year, unwinding most of the real spare capacity available to its members. With the OPEC+ group arguably out of real spare capacity available to bring online, and the EIA forecasting an 800,000 b/d decline in US crude oil output by the end of 2027, we now seem to be in a sustained oil market tightening phase.

In the absence of a wildcard event, such as Venezuela deploying its massive reserves to duplicate the effect that the US shale boom had on the global oil markets between 2010 and 2025, the global oil market will most likely gradually and sustainably tighten from here until a demand destruction event brings it back into balance. Keeping this in mind, I intend to start buying Suncor shares incrementally on the way down, if its share price drops below $45. I intend to incrementally start taking profits if its share price rises above $55, while maintaining Suncor as one of my largest portfolio positions. In other words, I do not intend to fully liquidate my position.

My prior coverage of Suncor.

The last time I covered Suncor, I highlighted its solid Q3 financial results in the face of what we can consider to be a weak oil price environment. Given its strong share price performance and generous dividend, I see my position in this stock as the largest holding in my portfolio, as justified. I had the stock as a buy then, and I continue to have it as a buy now, even though it has briefly touched my $50/share price target at which I previously stated that I intend to start taking profits. My reasoning behind this move is that, aside from company-specific and oil market fundamentals, we should increasingly account for broader market fundamentals, where oil stocks are under-priced relative to the broader market.

A brief look at Suncor's company profile.

I covered Suncor's Q3 results in my last article, so I am not going to spend too much time focusing on it this time around. Its upstream production increased by 8% compared with the previous quarter, reaching a new record of 870,000 b/d. Net earnings improved from 1.13 billion Canadian dollars in Q2, to 1.62 billion. Revenues declined by 4% in the first nine months of 2025.

Within the context of declining oil prices, Suncor's financial performance held up decently. It sets the stage for its share price to surpass the all-time highs it reached in 2008 of $71/share, if I am correct about the potential for a sustained oil price rise starting in the second half of this year.

As the chart shows, not only did Suncor's share price advance significantly in the past year, even as oil prices declined over 20% for the period, but it also outperformed its oil major peers, all of which advanced in the past year. I should note that the returns represented in the chart above include the dividend effect.

Oil producers' stocks are defying oil price trends.

It can be argued that the reason why oil producers gained in share price in the past year, even as oil prices declined, is that the market is forward-looking and, as such, it is betting on higher oil prices in the future by positioning for it now. As I shall explain, I am one of the investors betting on higher oil prices in the future, based on the assumption that the oil market is set to start tightening from this point forward.

Another, less obvious reason for oil producers to rally is the fact that the broader stock markets are trading at rising valuation levels.

With stock markets trading at roughly double the P/E ratio they have historically, it is reasonable to expect oil producers to start trading at a higher P/E ratio as well. Suncor is currently trading at a P/E ratio of 16, whereas a year ago it was trading at a P/E ratio of about 10. It still has a lower P/E ratio compared to ExxonMobil (XOM), which is trading at a forward ratio of 19, and Chevron (CVX), at 23. Shell (SHEL) is trading at a forward P/E ratio of around 12, which is lower than Suncor's, but this is arguably due to its very poor upstream reserves situation.

The net effect of the market's rising P/E ratio on all sectors of the market is to lift all boats higher. The oil sector in particular may rise at a faster pace compared with the broader markets, because investors are increasingly looking for bargains within a historically overpriced market, while low oil prices make for a favorable entry point, if one assumes higher oil prices in the future. I foresee a slight convergence between oil producer P/E ratios and the market's overall P/E ratio, which itself seems to be poised to keep rising.

Global oil market balance to see steady tightening.

The world is currently experiencing a period of oil market surplus, with the actual volume up for institutional debate. OPEC sees a more or less balanced global market, while the EIA sees a sizable 2.5 mb/d surplus currently, mostly due to US output rising by about 700,000 b/d, and OPEC+ raising output by over 2 mb/d last year. The IEA sees a world that is currently drowning in crude oil, which will continue to be the case for the foreseeable future. All three institutions cannot be correct, since there is such a sizable gap between OPEC on one hand and the EIA & IEA on the other.

My personal view is that we might find reality somewhere in the middle, in between the two opposed institutional camps. In other words, there is most likely an oil output surplus on the global market, but it might not be as great as the likes of EIA are claiming. Furthermore, we most likely entered a period of sustained global oil market tightening at the start of this year.

The EIA is forecasting a drop of about 800,000 b/d in US oil output between the fourth quarter of 2025 and the fourth quarter of 2027. OPEC+ is credited with still having as much as 4-5 mb/d in spare capacity, which is pure fiction in my view. Interestingly, the EIA & IEA both failed to significantly reduce the spare capacity estimates for OPEC members, even though they brought a significant volume of spare capacity back into production last year.

As we can see, the EIA believes that OPEC currently has about 3.5 mb/d in spare capacity this year, after last year's increase in output, based on their latest monthly report.

In its January 2025 report it had OPEC spare capacity forecast at 4 mb/d for this year, after the organization added about 1.5 mb/d in production last year, and there are few indications that OPEC members managed to significantly add to their production capacity last year or that they will do so this year.

Regardless of how we look at this data, it seems that the EIA is reluctant to fully subtract OPEC's additions from its spare capacity estimates. Those estimates are historically inflated, with examples such as Saudi Arabia and other OPEC members caught in 2008 unable to meaningfully increase oil output from their claimed spare capacity, which led to the all-time high record in oil prices that year.

OPEC+ is unlikely to increase output this year or next. Any spare capacity that some members may still hold is most likely to be kept in reserve in the event of a significant global oil supply disruption, which would require them to demonstrate their ability to react. At the same time, US oil output growth that kept the world from stagnating oil production between 2010 and 2025 is now headed in reverse, with output set to steadily decline by about 100,000 b/d every quarter or so. Global oil demand is still increasing by about 1 million barrels per day (mb/d) every year. I see oil prices responding to this situation of market tightening in the second half of this year, when the supply/demand balance will look significantly different, assuming that oil demand continues to grow as expected.

Investment implications.

With institutions forecasting a world that is oversupplied with oil and will continue to be for the foreseeable future, and widespread acceptance of those forecasts, it may be difficult for many investors to see the value of Suncor from my vantage point. My view is that the temporary supply surplus created in 2025 is gradually dissipating, with demand growth on one end, and several countries set to see a decline in output on the other end, resulting in a relatively fast rebalancing of the global supply/demand equation, which will be followed by a swing into deepening supply deficits.

Within this context, Suncor is an ideal investment candidate within the oil sector, mostly because it has a comparatively superior upstream reserve situation.

It is currently increasing its upstream production. It reached a new all-time record high in Q3, with output expected to be higher in 2026 on the upper end of the forecast by about 2% compared with the upper end of the estimate for this year. It has a proven ability to profitably produce oil at current market prices.

With a higher P/E ratio due to its share price appreciating significantly in the past year, the dividend offered to investors buying shares at the current price level is no longer as generous, at about 3.5%, however, I think it is still worth buying this year on any significant dips, on expectations of its share price rising from here. I am looking to start buying only if it dips below $45/share, which is higher than my prior $40/share target I had in place a few months back. I am looking to take profits incrementally, if or when its share price rises above $55/share.

I might consider reducing my current sizable position in this stock if it rises above the all-time high reached in 2008, of over $70/share, but I will probably maintain a position in this stock regardless of how high its share price rises. My average cost basis for this stock is about $23/share, therefore, its current dividend expected to be $1.73/share this year is rather generous from my perspective.

Risks to my thesis.

As I pointed out in a recent article, the move by the Trump administration to replace the Maduro government in Venezuela may have the net effect of delaying a global peak in crude oil output by perhaps decades. This means that if all goes according to plan, oil prices may rise from current levels, but the oil market will mostly remain stable, with somewhat limited upside. In this event, Suncor's potential upside may be somewhat limited as well. Venezuelan oil output growth will require higher oil prices on a sustained basis from current levels; therefore, even if Venezuela increases oil output considerably, things are still somewhat positive for Suncor, since oil sands breakeven levels are lower than the price needed to extract Venezuela's oil, therefore a new price floor, at a higher level than the current one will form.

A demand destruction event, due to an unforeseen factor, as we saw during the COVID crisis, or perhaps due to a slowdown in the global economy, is always a persistent and potent risk factor for the oil industry. Oil is an inelastic good; therefore, it does not take much of a supply shortfall to make market prices spike, or much of a demand destruction event to have prices plunge. If we do see a demand destruction event, I intend to use it as a buying opportunity.

Conclusions.

Until recently, I mostly focused my buy thesis for Suncor on the obvious fundamentals that directly impact its financial performance, thus its share price. I mostly overlooked the likely effect that we can see from the overall market continuing to see a rise in P/E ratios, which inevitably leads to oil companies also seeing a rise in their P/E ratio. Since companies like Suncor are trading at a P/E ratio that is less than half compared with the broader markets, there is arguably more room for oil companies to see their stock price appreciate going forward than there is for the broader markets.

Aside from the broader market dynamics, the future performance of oil company stocks will depend on internal factors, as well as external oil market dynamics going forward. Since I last covered Suncor, the EIA came out with its first monthly report of the year, which for the first time includes forecasts for 2027. Based on that report, US crude oil output is set to decline by 800,000 b/d from Q4, 2025 levels, while global demand for oil will increase by about 2.4 mb/d this year and next.

These two factors alone amount to a market tightening by about 3.2 mb/d by the end of 2027. There are, of course, places like Canada, Guyana, or Argentina where we might see an increase in oil production, but there are also other places like Norway, which is set to see its output shrink significantly in coming years.

Suncor stands out as one of the best-positioned companies to benefit from a sustained tightening of the global oil markets, along with oil sands peers such as CNQ (CNQ). It has ample reserves that it can produce at reasonable breakeven costs, to support continued output growth, thus, continued improvement in its financial results for the longer term.
